era_context: Early Bronze Age III in the Levant and Mesopotamia. Egypt's Old Kingdom (Dynasty IV–V) continues under pharaonic rule. Mesopotamian city-states remain in competition. The Indus Valley Civilization is in its Early Harappan phase. This year falls within a period of relative stability in Egypt and the emergence of complex state structures in Mesopotamia and South Asia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia beyond the Indus Valley

events.title: Continuation of Old Kingdom Egypt under Dynasty IV
events.description: Egypt's Old Kingdom continues under the rule of Dynasty IV pharaohs. This period is characterized by the construction of monumental pyramids, the consolidation of centralized state power, and the development of a sophisticated bureaucracy. The year 2901 BCE falls within this era of pharaonic dominance, though no specific events are attested for this particular year.

sources.name: Turin King List and Manetho's Aegyptiaca
Archaeological evidence from Old Kingdom sites

events.certainty_note: No specific events are documented for the year 2901 BCE. This entry describes the broader era context. Absolute dating of the Old Kingdom is uncertain by ±50–100 years.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are attested for 2901 BCE, so no disconfirming evidence can be identified. The broader chronology of the Old Kingdom remains debated among Egyptologists.
historiographic_note: The year 2901 BCE has no documented events in the surviving historical record. The historical record for this period is dominated by Egyptian and Mesopotamian sources, with the rest of the world essentially invisible to written history. The absence of specific events for this year reflects the general sparseness of year-by-year documentation in the third millennium BCE.
